items.title: Always compute the evidence P(B) explicitly
items.avoid: Plugging P(B) = P(B|A) or skipping the denominator when multiple hypotheses exist.
items.prefer: Use the law of total probability: P(B) = Σᵢ P(B|Aᵢ)P(Aᵢ) over a complete partition.
items.why: The denominator normalizes posteriors and accounts for all ways evidence B can arise.
items.example: For spam/ham: `p_flag = p_f_spam * p_spam + p_f_ham * (1 - p_spam)` before `p_spam_given_flag = p_f_spam * p_spam / p_flag`.

items.title: Label hypothesis vs evidence before calculating
items.avoid: Memorizing the formula without assigning meaning to A and B.
items.prefer: Write down: A = what you want to know; B = what you observed. Then map P(B|A) to a known likelihood.
items.why: Correct labeling prevents swapping conditionals—the most common Bayes error in practice.
items.example: Medical: A = disease, B = positive test. ML triage: A = true violation, B = model flag.

items.title: Sanity-check posteriors against base rates
items.avoid: Accepting a posterior of 95% for a condition that affects 0.01% of the population without checking arithmetic.
items.prefer: Compare P(A|B) to P(A); verify the update direction makes sense given how diagnostic B is.
items.why: Rare priors cap achievable posteriors unless likelihood ratios are extreme.
items.example: If P(A)=0.001 and P(A|B)=0.90, recheck whether you swapped P(B|A) and P(A|B).
